title: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism

items.subtopic: Deep-Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: PE Mortality Trends in the U.S. vs. Europe
items.content: Pulmonary embolism (PE) mortality trends differ between the United States and Europe, with a plateau observed in the U.S. while continuing to decrease in Europe.
keyPoints: In the U.S., PE-related mortality decreased from 6 per 100,000 in 2000 to approximately 4.5 per 100,000 by 2017.
Europe's age-standardized annual PE-related mortality rate has continued to decrease linearly since 2000.
The median age at death from PE in the U.S. decreased from 73 years in 2000 to 68 years in 2018.
PE-related mortality is increasing among young and middle-aged adults in the U.S.
Annual PE mortality was up to 50% higher in African Americans compared with Caucasians.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · Deep-Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Deep-Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Socioeconomic Disparities in PE Hospitalizations and Mortality
items.content: PE hospitalizations have increased across socioeconomic groups, with higher mortality rates observed in socioeconomically disadvantaged areas.
keyPoints: Hospitalizations for MI, heart failure, and stroke declined in both socioeconomically disadvantaged and nondisadvantaged communities.
In contrast, PE hospitalizations increased in both disadvantaged and nondisadvantaged communities.
By 2019, 30-day mortality was similar between hospitalized beneficiaries from socioeconomically disadvantaged and nondisadvantaged communities for MI, heart failure, and ischemic stroke.
Mortality among patients hospitalized with PE was consistently higher in disadvantaged areas, potentially due to poor outpatient follow-up and impeded access to anticoagulant medications.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · Deep-Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Inflammation as a Trigger for VTE
items.content: Inflammation plays a central role in triggering acute pulmonary embolism (PE) and deep venous thrombosis (DVT). Inflammation-related risk factors and medical illnesses are now recognized as precipitants of venous thromboembolism (VTE).
keyPoints: Inflammation is a key trigger for acute PE and DVT.
Inflammation-related risk factors and medical conditions can precipitate VTE.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Prothrombotic States and Genetic Mutations
items.content: The two most common autosomal dominant genetic mutations associated with VTE are factor V Leiden, which causes resistance to protein C, and the prothrombin gene mutation, which increases plasma prothrombin concentration. Antithrombin, protein C, and protein S deficiencies are rare but linked to VTE.
keyPoints: Factor V Leiden leads to resistance to protein C.
Prothrombin gene mutation increases plasma prothrombin levels.
Antithrombin, protein C, and protein S deficiencies are associated with VTE but are rare.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Polygenic Risk Scores for VTE
items.content: A genome-wide association study identified 93 risk loci for VTE, affecting coagulation and platelet function. A polygenic risk score (PRS) can identify high- and low-risk individuals, with those in the top 0.1% having a similar risk to homozygous carriers of factor V Leiden or prothrombin gene mutation.
keyPoints: 93 risk loci for VTE were identified, affecting coagulation and platelet function.
Top 0.1% of PRS distribution have VTE risk similar to homozygous carriers of factor V Leiden or prothrombin gene mutation.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | CLASSIFICATION OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM AND DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.title: Mechanisms and Consequences of Right Ventricular Dysfunction in Pulmonary Embolism
items.content: Pulmonary embolism can lead to pulmonary hypertension, which increases pulmonary artery pressure and resistance. This causes right ventricular (RV) wall tension to rise, leading to RV dilation, dysfunction, and the release of cardiac biomarkers like brain natriuretic peptide and troponin. The interventricular septum may bulge into the left ventricle, causing diastolic dysfunction and reduced LV filling. Increased RV wall tension can compress the right coronary artery, reducing myocardial oxygen supply and potentially leading to RV microinfarction.
keyPoints: Pulmonary hypertension increases pulmonary artery pressure and resistance.
RV dysfunction leads to release of brain natriuretic peptide and troponin.
Interventricular septum bulging compresses the left ventricle, reducing LV filling.
Increased RV wall tension can cause right coronary artery ischemia and RV microinfarction.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | CLASSIFICATION OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM AND DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | CLASSIFICATION OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM AND DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.title: Classification of Pulmonary Embolism by Risk Level and Clinical Features
items.content: Pulmonary embolisms are classified into massive, submassive, and low-risk categories. Massive PE, accounting for 5–10% of cases, is characterized by systemic arterial hypotension and extensive thrombosis affecting at least half of the pulmonary vasculature, with symptoms like dyspnea, syncope, hypotension, and cyanosis. Submassive PE (20–25%) involves RV dysfunction without systemic hypotension but indicates a high risk of clinical deterioration due to right heart failure and troponin release. Low-risk PE (65–75%) has an excellent prognosis.
keyPoints: Massive PE: 5-10% of cases, characterized by systemic arterial hypotension and extensive thrombosis.
Submassive PE: 20-25% of cases, involves RV dysfunction with normal systemic pressure but high risk due to troponin release.
Low-risk PE: 65-75% of cases, excellent prognosis.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | CLASSIFICATION OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM AND DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.title: D-dimer Testing in PE Diagnosis
items.content: D-dimer testing is a highly sensitive test for pulmonary embolism (PE) with a sensitivity of >95%. It serves as an effective 'rule out' test for patients with low pretest probability, but it lacks specificity due to elevated levels in various conditions such as MI, pneumonia, sepsis, cancer, and postoperative states.
keyPoints: D-dimer sensitivity for PE is >95%.
A normal D-dimer can rule out PE in patients with low pretest probability.
Elevated D-dimer levels are non-specific and can occur in MI, pneumonia, sepsis, cancer, postoperative states, and pregnancy.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Age-Adjusted D-dimer for PE
items.content: The age-adjusted D-dimer threshold is used to improve diagnostic accuracy in patients over 50 years old with low or intermediate clinical probability of PE. The upper limit is calculated by multiplying the patient's age by 10, reducing unnecessary CT pulmonary angiograms.
keyPoints: Age-adjusted D-dimer: multiply age by 10 for patients >50 with low/intermediate PE probability.
Example: A 70-year-old has an upper limit of 700 ng/mL.
Reduces unnecessary CT pulmonary angiograms.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Evaluation and Differential Diagnosis in PE
items.content: PE is often difficult to diagnose due to nonspecific symptoms. Unexplained breathlessness, especially when not improving with treatment for other conditions like heart failure or pneumonia, suggests possible PE. Not all dyspnea indicates PE; differential diagnoses include fat embolism, tumor embolism, and amniotic fluid embolism.
keyPoints: PE symptoms are nonspecific, often presenting as unexplained breathlessness.
Clinical improvement not seen with standard treatment for heart failure or pneumonia may indicate PE.
Differential diagnoses include fat embolism, tumor embolism, and amniotic fluid embolism.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Ultrasonography for Diagnosing Acute DVT
items.content: Ultrasonography is the primary diagnostic tool for acute deep venous thrombosis (DVT) due to its ability to assess vein compressibility. A normal vein collapses under gentle pressure, but an acutely thrombosed vein does not, indicating DVT.
keyPoints: Loss of vein compressibility indicates DVT.
Direct visualization of homogeneous, low echogenicity thrombus confirms diagnosis.
Doppler imaging can assess venous flow dynamics; loss of respiratory variation suggests obstructive processes.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Chest CT for Diagnosing Pulmonary Embolism
items.content: CT pulmonary angiography with intravenous contrast is the principal imaging test for diagnosing pulmonary embolism (PE). It provides detailed images and can reveal right ventricular enlargement, which correlates with increased mortality risk.
keyPoints: Thin-cut chest CT offers ≤1 mm resolution during a short breath hold.
RV enlargement on CT indicates higher mortality risk; RV/LV diameter ratio >1 is concerning.
CT identifies alternative diagnoses like pneumonia or pulmonary fibrosis.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Ventilation/Perfusion Scanning for PE
items.content: Ventilation/perfusion lung scanning is a second-line diagnostic test for PE, especially useful when intravenous contrast is contraindicated. It involves assessing perfusion defects and ventilation patterns to diagnose or rule out PE.
keyPoints: High-probability scan: two or more segmental perfusion defects with normal ventilation (~90% certainty of PE).
Normal scans make PE very unlikely.
Ventilation scans help differentiate causes of perfusion defects, such as asthma or COPD.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Preventing Upper Extremity DVT with PICC Line Management
items.content: The incidence of upper extremity deep venous thrombosis (DVT) can be mitigated by careful patient selection for peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC) use, opting for single-lumen over multi-lumen PICCs, and choosing the smallest feasible lumen size.
keyPoints: Increased PICC usage correlates with higher rates of upper extremity DVT.
Use a 4 French lumen instead of larger sizes to decrease risk.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · UPPER EXTREMITY DVT | ISOLATED CALF DVT | FIBRINOLYSIS | COMPRESSION STOCKINGS

items.title: Evaluating the Role of Catheter-Directed Thrombolysis in Postthrombotic Syndrome Prevention
items.content: Catheter-directed thrombolysis, when combined with anticoagulation, does not significantly reduce postthrombotic syndrome overall but shows a trend towards benefit specifically for patients with iliofemoral DVT.
keyPoints: ATTRACT trial involved 692 patients with femoral or iliofemoral DVT.
No overall reduction in postthrombotic syndrome after 2 years of follow-up.
Trend towards benefit observed for iliofemoral DVT, not just femoral.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · UPPER EXTREMITY DVT | ISOLATED CALF DVT | FIBRINOLYSIS | COMPRESSION STOCKINGS

items.title: Identifying High-Risk Pulmonary Embolism Patients
items.content: Patients with hemodynamic instability, RV dysfunction on echocardiography, RV enlargement on chest CT, and elevated troponin levels due to RV microinfarction are at high risk of adverse outcomes despite anticoagulation.
keyPoints: Hemodynamic instability, RV dysfunction, RV enlargement, and elevated troponin indicate high-risk PE.
Normal RV function and hemodynamics suggest excellent prognosis.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · RISK STRATIFICATION | ANTICOAGULATION (part 1)

items.title: Anticoagulation Strategies for VTE
items.content: Three major strategies include parenteral anticoagulation with UFH, LMWH, or fondaparinux bridged to warfarin; switching from parenteral anticoagulation to DOACs like dabigatran or edoxaban after 5 days; and oral monotherapy with rivaroxaban or apixaban.
keyPoints: UFH targets aPTT of 60–80 s, corresponding to anti-Xa levels of 0.3–0.7 U/mL.
LMWH requires no monitoring unless the patient is markedly obese or has chronic kidney disease.
Fondaparinux does not require laboratory monitoring and is adjusted for renal dysfunction.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · RISK STRATIFICATION | ANTICOAGULATION (part 1)

items.title: Dosing Guidelines for DOACs
items.content: DOACs like rivaroxaban, apixaban, dabigatran, and edoxaban have specific dosing regimens based on renal function and patient characteristics.
keyPoints: Rivaroxaban: 15 mg twice daily for 3 weeks, then 20 mg once daily; adjust to 10 mg twice daily for CrCl 15–50 mL/min.
Apixaban: 10 mg twice daily for 1 week, then 5 mg twice daily; reduce to 2.5 mg if age >80 years, weight <60 kg, or serum creatinine >1.5 mg/dL.
Dabigatran: 150 mg twice daily with CrCl >30 mL/min; adjust to 75 mg for CrCl 15–30 mL/min.
Edoxaban: 60 mg once daily with normal renal function and weight >60 kg; reduce to 30 mg for CrCl 15–50 mL/min.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · RISK STRATIFICATION | ANTICOAGULATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTICOAGULATION (part 2) | TABLE 290-5 Take-Home Points from the European Society of Cardiology 2019 Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines
items.title: Guidelines for Extended Anticoagulation in First Episode PE
items.content: The ESC guidelines recommend considering extended oral anticoagulation without an end date for patients experiencing a first episode of pulmonary embolism (PE) under certain conditions. This includes cases with no identifiable risk factor, persistent risk factors, or minor transient/reversible risk factors.
keyPoints: Extended anticoagulation should be considered for first-episode PE with no identifiable risk factor, persistent risk factor, or minor transient/reversible risk factor.
The terminology 'provoked' vs. 'unprovoked' is not supported as it does not aid in decision-making regarding the duration of anticoagulation.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · ANTICOAGULATION (part 2) | TABLE 290-5 Take-Home Points from the European Society of Cardiology 2019 Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines | INFERIOR VENA CAVA FILTERS

items.title: Indications for Inferior Vena Cava Filter Placement
items.content: Inferior vena cava filters are primarily indicated for active bleeding that precludes anticoagulation and recurrent venous thrombosis despite intensive anticoagulation. They may also be used to prevent PE in patients who cannot receive anticoagulation, although this is a weaker indication.
keyPoints: Principal indications: (1) Active bleeding preventing anticoagulation; (2) Recurrent venous thrombosis despite anticoagulation.
Filters may increase DVT risk by providing a nidus for clot formation, though they usually prevent PE.
Retrievable filters are preferred over permanent ones in most situations.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · ANTICOAGULATION (part 2) | TABLE 290-5 Take-Home Points from the European Society of Cardiology 2019 Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines | INFERIOR VENA CAVA FILTERS

items.title: Fluid Management and Vasopressor Use in Massive PE
items.content: In managing massive pulmonary embolism (PE) with hypotension, initial fluid resuscitation should be limited to 500 mL of normal saline. Excessive fluids can worsen right ventricular (RV) stress and ischemia by increasing interventricular septal shift toward the left ventricle (LV), impairing LV compliance and filling. Norepinephrine and dobutamine are preferred for vasopressor support, with norepinephrine raising systemic arterial pressure and dobutamine enhancing RV inotropy while reducing filling pressures.
keyPoints: Initial fluid resuscitation should be limited to 500 mL of normal saline.
Excessive fluids worsen RV stress by increasing interventricular septal shift toward the LV.
Norepinephrine increases systemic arterial pressure.
Dobutamine enhances RV inotropy and lowers filling pressures.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · MANAGEMENT OF MASSIVE PE | FIBRINOLYSIS

items.title: Fibrinolytic Therapy for Massive PE
items.content: For patients with massive PE, fibrinolysis using 100 mg of recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) administered as a continuous intravenous infusion over 2 hours is FDA-approved. This therapy rapidly reverses right heart failure and may reduce mortality and recurrence by dissolving obstructive thrombus in pulmonary arteries, preventing serotonin release that exacerbates pulmonary hypertension, and lysing the source of thrombus. Systemic fibrinolysis can be effective up to 14 days post-PE. A lower dose regimen (50 mg over 2 hours) is less effective but may reduce bleeding risks.
keyPoints: FDA-approved dosage for massive PE: 100 mg tPA administered over 2 hours.
Fibrinolysis can be effective up to 14 days after PE occurrence.
Lower dose (50 mg over 2 hours) is less effective but may reduce bleeding complications.
Major bleeding rate with fibrinolysis is ~10%, including a 2–3% risk of intracranial hemorrhage.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · MANAGEMENT OF MASSIVE PE | FIBRINOLYSIS

items.subtopic: CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT
items.title: Understanding Catheter-Directed Therapy for VTE
items.content: Catheter-directed therapy offers a targeted approach to treat venous thromboembolism (VTE) with three main strategies: pharmacologic, mechanical, and pharmacomechanical. Pharmacologic involves low-dose continuous infusion of tissue plasminogen activator (tPA). Mechanical therapy employs percutaneous thrombectomy using devices like expandable nitinol disks to trap and remove thrombus without thrombolytics. Pharmacomechanical combines low-energy ultrasound with low-dose thrombolysis, reducing major bleeding complications compared to systemic fibrinolysis.
keyPoints: Three types of catheter-directed therapy: pharmacologic, mechanical, and pharmacomechanical.
Pharmacologic uses low-dose continuous infusion of tPA.
Mechanical employs suction, snaring, and extraction without thrombolytics; devices include expandable nitinol disks.
Pharmacomechanical combines low-energy ultrasound with low-dose thrombolysis.
Lower major bleeding complication rate compared to systemic fibrinolysis.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT

items.subtopic: CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT
items.title: Role and Outcomes of Surgical Pulmonary Embolectomy
items.content: Surgical pulmonary embolectomy is a viable alternative to systemic fibrinolysis, especially given the risk of major hemorrhage. The procedure has seen renewed interest due to improved surgical techniques, rapid referral processes before irreversible organ failure, and use of extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO). These advancements have increased survival rates and significantly enhanced right ventricular function.
keyPoints: Surgical embolectomy is an alternative to systemic fibrinolysis due to hemorrhage risks.
Improved surgical techniques and rapid referral enhance outcomes.
Use of ECMO has improved survival rates and right ventricular function.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT

items.question: Which genetic mutation causes resistance to protein C, a naturally occurring anticoagulant?
items.explanation: Factor V Leiden causes resistance to the endogenous anticoagulant, protein C.

items.question: What is a common clinical risk factor for venous thromboembolism that involves lifestyle?
items.explanation: A sedentary lifestyle is an increasingly prevalent risk factor for VTE.

items.question: What condition predisposes to both venous and arterial thrombosis and often requires lifetime anticoagulation?
items.explanation: Antiphospholipid syndrome is an acquired thrombophilic disorder that predisposes to both venous and arterial thrombosis.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is associated with a broad interaction between VTE and acute coronary syndrome?
items.explanation: The presence of carotid artery plaque is associated with a broad interaction between VTE and acute coronary syndrome.

items.question: Which of the following is a hallmark symptom of massive pulmonary embolism?
items.explanation: Dyspnea, along with syncope, hypotension, and cyanosis, is a hallmark of massive pulmonary embolism.

items.question: What characterizes submassive pulmonary embolism?
options: Normal systemic arterial pressure with RV dysfunction
Systemic arterial hypotension and extensive thrombosis
items.explanation: Submassive pulmonary embolism is characterized by right ventricular dysfunction despite normal systemic arterial pressure.

items.question: What is the most common symptom of pulmonary embolism (PE)?
items.explanation: The passage states that unexplained breathlessness is the most common symptom of PE.

Skip D-dimer testing and proceed to treatment
items.explanation: Patients with a low likelihood of DVT or a low-to-moderate likelihood of PE should undergo initial diagnostic evaluation with D-dimer testing alone if the result is normal.

items.question: What does an elevated D-dimer level indicate in patients with suspected PE?

It is specific but insensitive for PE

items.question: What imaging finding on ultrasonography is considered a primary diagnostic criterion for deep venous thrombosis (DVT)?
items.explanation: Ultrasonography for DVT relies on loss of vein compressibility as the primary diagnostic criterion.

items.question: Which sign on chest CT is associated with an increased likelihood of death within 30 days in patients with pulmonary embolism (PE)?
items.explanation: RV enlargement on chest CT, indicated by Palla's sign, is associated with an increased likelihood of death within the next 30 days.

options: CT pulmonary angiography with intravenous contrast
items.explanation: CT pulmonary angiography with intravenous contrast is the principal imaging test for diagnosing PE.

items.question: Which medication is an effective, rapidly acting antidote for dabigatran?
items.explanation: Idarucizumab is the specific antidote for dabigatran.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · ANTICOAGULATION (part 2) | TABLE 290-5 Take-Home Points from the European Society of Cardiology 2019 Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines | INFERIOR VENA CAVA FILTERS

items.subtopic: ANTICOAGULATION (part 2) | TABLE 290-5 Take-Home Points from the European Society of Cardiology 2019 Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines | INFERIOR VENA CAVA FILTERS
items.question: According to the guidelines, what should be considered for patients with a first episode of PE and no identifiable risk factor?
options: Extended oral anticoagulation without an end date
items.explanation: Extended oral anticoagulation without an end date is recommended for patients with no identifiable risk factor.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · ANTICOAGULATION (part 2) | TABLE 290-5 Take-Home Points from the European Society of Cardiology 2019 Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines | INFERIOR VENA CAVA FILTERS

items.subtopic: ANTICOAGULATION (part 2) | TABLE 290-5 Take-Home Points from the European Society of Cardiology 2019 Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines | INFERIOR VENA CAVA FILTERS
items.question: Which two principal indications are there for inserting an inferior vena cava filter?
options: Active bleeding that precludes anticoagulation and prevention of PE in patients who can take anticoagulants
Recurrent venous thrombosis despite intensive anticoagulation and active bleeding that precludes anticoagulation
Prevention of DVT in high-risk surgical patients and recurrent venous thrombosis despite intensive anticoagulation
Active bleeding that precludes anticoagulation and prevention of PE in patients who are not candidates for anticoagulation
items.explanation: The two principal indications are active bleeding that precludes anticoagulation and recurrent venous thrombosis despite intensive anticoagulation.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · ANTICOAGULATION (part 2) | TABLE 290-5 Take-Home Points from the European Society of Cardiology 2019 Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines | INFERIOR VENA CAVA FILTERS

items.question: Which agent is recommended as a first-line vasopressor for the treatment of PE-related shock?
items.explanation: Norepinephrine increases systemic arterial pressure and is recommended as a first-line vasopressor for PE-related shock.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · MANAGEMENT OF MASSIVE PE | FIBRINOLYSIS

items.question: What is the FDA-approved dose of recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) for massive PE?
items.explanation: The FDA-approved dose of tPA for massive PE is 100 mg administered as a continuous intravenous infusion over 2 hours.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · MANAGEMENT OF MASSIVE PE | FIBRINOLYSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a contraindication to fibrinolysis in patients with PE?
items.explanation: Hypotension is not listed as a contraindication to fibrinolysis; intracranial disease, recent surgery, and trauma are.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · MANAGEMENT OF MASSIVE PE | FIBRINOLYSIS

items.subtopic: CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a type of catheter-directed therapy for treating VTE?
items.explanation: The passage describes three types of catheter-directed therapy: pharmacologic only, mechanical only, and pharmacomechanical. Ultrasound-guided thrombolysis is not mentioned as a separate type.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT

items.subtopic: CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT
items.question: What is the mortality rate for pulmonary thromboendarterectomy at experienced centers?
items.explanation: The passage states that the mortality rate at experienced centers for pulmonary thromboendarterectomy is 1–5%.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT

items.subtopic: CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT
items.question: Which of the following is a benefit of low-dose thrombolysis in pharmacomechanical therapy compared to systemically administered fibrinolysis?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that the lower major bleeding complication rate is a benefit of low-dose thrombolysis compared to systemically administered fibrinolysis.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT

items.subtopic: Deep-Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: PE-related mortality in the United States has shown a linear decrease since 2000.
items.explanation: PE-related mortality decreased from 6 per 100,000 in 2000 and plateaued at approximately 4.5 per 100,000 by 2017.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · Deep-Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Deep-Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: Hospitalizations for PE have increased in socioeconomically disadvantaged communities.
items.explanation: PE hospitalizations increased in both disadvantaged and nondisadvantaged communities.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · Deep-Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Antithrombin, protein C, and protein S deficiencies are common causes of VTE.
items.explanation: Deficiencies of antithrombin, protein C, and protein S are associated with VTE but are rare.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: Patients with a high polygenic risk score for VTE have a similar risk to those carrying factor V Leiden.
items.explanation: Individuals within the top 0.1% of PRS distribution had a VTE risk similar to homozygous or compound heterozygous carriers of factor V Leiden.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | CLASSIFICATION OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM AND DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.statement: Massive pulmonary embolism accounts for about 65–75% of cases.
items.explanation: Massive PE accounts for only 5–10% of cases, while low-risk PE constitutes about 65–75%.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | CLASSIFICATION OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM AND DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | CLASSIFICATION OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM AND DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.statement: Superficial venous thrombosis can extend to the deep-venous system.
items.explanation: Patients with superficial venous thrombosis are at risk for extension to the deep-venous system.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | CLASSIFICATION OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM AND DEEP-VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.statement: A normal D-dimer test can rule out PE in patients with high pretest probability.
items.explanation: A normal D-dimer is useful for ruling out PE only in patients with low pretest probability.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: The age-adjusted D-dimer applies to all patients suspected of acute VTE.
items.explanation: The age-adjusted D-dimer does not apply to patients suspected of acute DVT.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: A normal chest x-ray rules out the possibility of pulmonary embolism.
items.explanation: A normal or nearly normal chest x-ray can occur in PE, so it does not rule out the condition.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.statement: Echocardiography is a reliable diagnostic tool for detecting acute pulmonary embolism.
items.explanation: Most patients with PE have normal echocardiograms, making it an unreliable diagnostic tool for acute PE.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: UPPER EXTREMITY DVT | ISOLATED CALF DVT | FIBRINOLYSIS | COMPRESSION STOCKINGS
items.statement: Most patients with isolated calf DVT received anticoagulation treatment for at least one year.
items.explanation: Nearly half of the isolated calf DVT patients were anticoagulated for at least 1 year.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · UPPER EXTREMITY DVT | ISOLATED CALF DVT | FIBRINOLYSIS | COMPRESSION STOCKINGS

items.subtopic: UPPER EXTREMITY DVT | ISOLATED CALF DVT | FIBRINOLYSIS | COMPRESSION STOCKINGS
items.statement: Compression stockings prevent postthrombotic syndrome in asymptomatic newly diagnosed acute DVT patients.
items.explanation: Prescription of compression stockings does not prevent the development of postthrombotic syndrome in these patients.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · UPPER EXTREMITY DVT | ISOLATED CALF DVT | FIBRINOLYSIS | COMPRESSION STOCKINGS

items.statement: DOACs require laboratory coagulation monitoring.
items.explanation: DOACs do not require laboratory coagulation monitoring.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · RISK STRATIFICATION | ANTICOAGULATION (part 1)

items.statement: Warfarin is the standard treatment for APS.
items.explanation: Warfarin, not DOACs, is the standard treatment for antiphospholipid syndrome (APS).
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · RISK STRATIFICATION | ANTICOAGULATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTICOAGULATION (part 2) | TABLE 290-5 Take-Home Points from the European Society of Cardiology 2019 Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines | INFERIOR VENA CAVA FILTERS
items.statement: The term 'provoked' versus 'unprovoked' PE/DVT is supported by current guidelines for determining anticoagulation duration.
items.explanation: Current guidelines no longer support the terminology of 'provoked' versus 'unprovoked' as it is misleading.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · ANTICOAGULATION (part 2) | TABLE 290-5 Take-Home Points from the European Society of Cardiology 2019 Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines | INFERIOR VENA CAVA FILTERS

items.subtopic: ANTICOAGULATION (part 2) | TABLE 290-5 Take-Home Points from the European Society of Cardiology 2019 Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines | INFERIOR VENA CAVA FILTERS
items.statement: Retrievable filters are preferred over permanent ones for most situations.
items.explanation: Retrievable filters are recommended in most situations due to their advantages.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · ANTICOAGULATION (part 2) | TABLE 290-5 Take-Home Points from the European Society of Cardiology 2019 Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines | INFERIOR VENA CAVA FILTERS

items.statement: Fibrinolytic therapy can be effectively used up to 14 days after a pulmonary embolism.
items.explanation: Systemic fibrinolysis can be utilized effectively for at least 14 days after the PE has occurred.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · MANAGEMENT OF MASSIVE PE | FIBRINOLYSIS

items.statement: Dobutamine is primarily used to increase systemic arterial pressure in patients with massive PE.
items.explanation: Dobutamine increases RV inotropy and lowers filling pressures, not primarily systemic arterial pressure.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · MANAGEMENT OF MASSIVE PE | FIBRINOLYSIS

items.subtopic: CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT
items.statement: CTEPH develops in about 10% of acute PE patients.
items.explanation: The passage states that CTEPH develops in about 2% of acute PE patients, not 10%.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT

items.subtopic: CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT
items.statement: A physician-nurse-facilitated PE support group at Brigham and Women's Hospital has been meeting monthly for over 30 years.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that a physician-nurse-facilitated PE support group at Brigham and Women's Hospital meets monthly and has done so for more than 30 years.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT

items.subtopic: Deep-Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.assertion: The median age at death from PE decreased from 73 years in 2000 to 68 years in 2018.
items.reason: PE-related mortality is increasing among young and middle-aged adults.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · Deep-Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Activated platelets release microparticles that stimulate neutrophils.
items.reason: These microparticles contain proinflammatory mediators that bind neutrophils, stimulating them to form extracellular networks.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Increased pulmonary vascular resistance is due to hypoxemia from alveolar hypoventilation.
items.reason: Release of vasoactive mediators can produce ventilation-perfusion mismatching at sites remote from the embolus.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason provided does not explain increased pulmonary vascular resistance; it explains ventilation-perfusion mismatching.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Pulmonary infarction usually indicates a small PE.
items.reason: The thrombus lodges peripherally, near the innervation of pleural nerves.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why pulmonary infarction is indicative of a small PE.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: The upper limit of normal for D-dimer should be considered as 500 ng/mL in all patients.
items.reason: Guidelines recommend an age-adjusted D-dimer when ruling out acute PE.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the guidelines now recommend using an age-adjusted D-dimer, not a fixed value of 500 ng/mL.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: A high-probability lung scan indicates a very likely diagnosis of pulmonary embolism.
items.reason: A high-probability scan is defined as two or more segmental perfusion defects with normal ventilation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why a high-probability scan indicates a likely diagnosis of PE.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.assertion: Contrast phlebography is the principal diagnostic test for suspected DVT.
items.reason: Venous ultrasonography has replaced contrast phlebography as the primary diagnostic tool for DVT.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because venous ultrasonography, not contrast phlebography, is the principal test; however, the reason is true.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: UPPER EXTREMITY DVT | ISOLATED CALF DVT | FIBRINOLYSIS | COMPRESSION STOCKINGS
items.assertion: The open vein hypothesis suggests that primary therapy can reduce long-term damage to venous valves.
items.reason: Patients receiving catheter-directed thrombolysis show a trend toward less postthrombotic syndrome with iliofemoral DVT compared to anticoagulation alone.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain the assertion.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · UPPER EXTREMITY DVT | ISOLATED CALF DVT | FIBRINOLYSIS | COMPRESSION STOCKINGS

items.assertion: Fondaparinux does not cause heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
items.reason: Fondaparinux is synthesized in a laboratory and is not derived from animal products.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why fondaparinux does not cause heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · RISK STRATIFICATION | ANTICOAGULATION (part 1)

items.assertion: The efficacy and safety of DOACs are well-established in patients with end-stage kidney disease.
items.reason: DOACs have few drug-drug interactions and do not require laboratory monitoring.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the efficacy and safety of DOACs remain uncertain in patients with end-stage kidney disease.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · RISK STRATIFICATION | ANTICOAGULATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT
items.assertion: Prevention of DVT and PE is crucial due to the difficulty in detection and significant medical burden.
items.reason: Low-dose UFH or LMWH is commonly used for in-hospital prophylaxis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why prevention of DVT and PE is crucial; it merely states a common preventive measure.
items.reference: Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Thromboembolism · CATHETER-DIRECTED THERAPY | SURGICAL PULMONARY EMBOLECTOMY | PULMONARY THROMBOENDARTERECTOMY | PREVENTION OF VTE | EMOTIONAL SUPPORT
